text:
10 BOOKS SERIOUS AI RESEARCHERS ACTUALLY RECOMMEND (NOT THE ONES EVERYONE POSTS)

Every AI reading list says the same five names. The people actually building these systems read deeper than that. Here's the shelf they point to when nobody's performing for an audience.

1. Probability Theory: The Logic of Science - E.T. Jaynes

The book researchers quietly call life-changing. Jaynes reframes probability not as gambling odds but as the mathematics of reasoning under uncertainty, which is exactly what every modern model is doing. Dense, opinionated, and the closest thing the field has to a sacred text. Almost nobody outside the work has heard of it.

2. Information Theory, Inference, and Learning Algorithms - David MacKay

The book that unites information theory and machine learning in one place, written by a Cambridge physicist who made it genuinely fun. Free online, full of puzzles, and on the shelf of nearly every researcher who actually understands why their models compress and predict the way they do.

3. Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction - Sutton and Barto

The foundation under everything from AlphaGo to how modern models get fine-tuned with human feedback. Researchers don't recommend it because it's trendy. They recommend it because the ideas in it keep turning out to be the ideas that matter, decades later. Also free.

4. The Book of Why - Judea Pearl

A Turing Award winner's argument that today's AI is stuck because it confuses correlation with causation, and a map of what real reasoning would require. The book that names the exact ceiling current systems keep hitting. Researchers cite it constantly. The public reads past it.

5. Vision - David Marr

A neuroscientist's framework for how any system, brain or machine, processes information, written before deep learning existed and somehow predicting the questions it would face. The "levels of analysis" idea in here quietly shapes how serious people think about what a model is even doing.

6. Gödel, Escher, Bach - Douglas Hofstadter

The cult book about how meaning and selfhood emerge from systems following simple rules. It won a Pulitzer and then got name-dropped to death, but almost nobody finishes it. The ones who do think differently about intelligence forever. The real one, not the summary.

7. Metaphors We Live By - Lakoff and Johnson

The argument that human thought runs on metaphor, not cold logic, and that you can't build a mind on first-order logic alone. Researchers working on why language models grasp meaning the strange way they do keep circling back to this one. A genuine left-field pick.

8. The Society of Mind - Marvin Minsky

One of AI's founding figures arguing that intelligence isn't one thing, it's a swarm of dumb little processes working together. Written as hundreds of one-page ideas. Out of fashion for years, now looking prophetic in the age of multi-agent systems. Insider catnip.

9. How to Solve It - George Pólya

A 1945 book on mathematical problem-solving that quietly shaped how a generation of researchers think about breaking down hard problems, and that keeps surfacing in papers on how to make models reason. The bridge between human heuristics and machine reasoning.

10. The Mathematical Theory of Communication - Claude Shannon

The original paper that invented information theory and, with it, the entire conceptual ground that machine learning stands on. Short, brutal, and foundational. Researchers revere Shannon the way physicists revere Newton. Most reading lists skip the source and quote the descendants.

The popular books tell you what AI might do. These tell you how the people building it actually think. The difference is the whole point.

- Fragments of Growth is a handcrafted art collection built from over a decade of travel, photography, textures, and natural light
- No generative AI

- Built from real-world surfaces, places, and moments captured across Europe and Asia
- Explores themes of memory, transformation, growth, and human experience

- The project turns fragmented moments into floral-inspired compositions
- Focused on artistic value and long-term collectibility rather than short-term hype
- Fine art collection rather than a traditional PFP project

DYOR & NFA.

AI app ideas are cheap. Launchable templates are the bottleneck.

Awesome Generative AI Apps is a curated GitHub repo of 50+ open-source generative AI app templates for builders who want to clone, deploy, and adapt AI SaaS products.

It helps you skip the blank Next.js project by grouping templates for image, video, beauty/fashion, e-commerce, writing, agents, audio, and integrations, with setup and deploy paths already documented.

Key features:

• 50+ app templates – cloneable products across image generation, video generation, virtual try-on, product photography, content, agents, and more
• SaaS plumbing included – README highlights Stripe billing, Google OAuth, Prisma/PostgreSQL, credit balances, and job history
• Vercel deployment path – templates point to Deploy to Vercel buttons and a quick-start flow
• Category navigation – table of contents lets you scan by use case instead of hunting through random demos
• MIT license – repo metadata and README both mark it as MIT licensed for reuse and adaptation

It’s open-source (MIT license).

Link in the reply 👇

🌸 Alpha - Fragments of Growth

A decade of travel, memories, and textures - now brought on-chain.

Created by fashion photographer and visual artist Jenya Vyguzov, the collection transforms textures captured across years of travel into handcrafted collage flowers , each petal representing a place, memory, or moment. No AI, no generative outputs, every piece is individually composed by hand.

What stands out here is the artist behind it. Jenya has spent over a decade working with brands like Vogue, GQ Style, Tatler, Elle, Puma, and IKEA, while building a distinctive visual language. His work was even selected for the official Paris Prints Textile Show poster, where he was given an entire floor for a solo exhibition.

⚠️WL applications are coming soon. If you're interested in art NFTs, this is the stage where you want to be paying attention. Follow the artist and the project, turn on notifications, and be ready when WL applications open.

dyor/nfa

Andrej Karpathy explains why RLHF needed a cheat code.

The naive version sounds simple: show humans model outputs and ask them to score which ones are good.

Then he does the math:

"I want to do 1,000 updates, where each update will be on 1,000 prompts, and for each prompt we're going to have 1,000 rollouts that we're scoring."

"I will need to ask a human to evaluate a joke a total of 1 billion times."

"That's a lot of people looking at really terrible jokes, so we don't want to do that."

The trick is indirection:

"We're going to involve humans just a little bit, and the way we cheat is that we basically train a whole separate neural network that we call a reward model."

That is the hidden abstraction behind RLHF:

Humans do not scale the feedback loop.
A model trained to imitate human preference scales it.

The new technical report on Krea 2 goes in depth on how to train production-level diffusion models, covering everything from data and infrastructure to RL post-training and distillation.

It also includes extensive ablation studies on design choices, covering recent diffusion model innovations ( TREAD, iREPA, ...), along with extensive architecture ablations inspired by recent language model architectures (GQA, MLA, gated sigmoid attention, ...).

Definitely worth reading!

I started working with generative AI about three years ago.
 Very quickly, fashion shows and Catwoman became my favorite themes.
Creating a character was very interesting back then, but also very laborious.
Today it's so much easier; it's accessible to everyone.
A tribute to my first favorite themes Grok Imagine 1.5 but with incredible quality and exemplary ease
